metadata.summary: Metrics framework, dashboard types, and KPI selection for project visibility across team, management, and executive audiences.

content.title: Reporting Basics
content.summary: Effective reporting transforms raw project data into actionable insights. This methodology covers metrics framework, dashboard types, and key performance indicators for project visibility.

why.p: Projects fail from information asymmetry — teams optimize for the wrong metrics, stakeholders lack visibility into delays until they are irreversible, and decisions are made on stale or incomplete data. Structured reporting with the right KPIs and dashboards for each audience (team, management, executive) closes the gap between execution and oversight.

list.item: Setting up new project visibility systems.
Improving existing reporting capabilities.
Creating stakeholder-specific dashboards.
Selecting key metrics and KPIs.
Replacing ad-hoc status emails with a recurring, audience-tailored dashboard.
Establishing a metrics framework before introducing OKRs / NorthStar reporting.
Auditing existing dashboards: which are read, which are stale, which to retire.
The team has fewer than ~5 issues per week — manual updates are faster than dashboards.
Highly research / discovery work where outputs are documents not tickets.
Pre-revenue founder mode: a daily Slack message is cheaper.

section.name: metrics-framework
section.p: Metrics organize into four categories: delivery, quality, flow, and team. Select 1-2 from each category; over-instrumentation produces noise.

section.name: delivery-metrics
section.p: Delivery metrics track output and predictability:

list.item: Velocity: Story points completed per sprint.
Throughput: Issues completed per time period.
Cycle time: Time from start to completion.
Lead time: Time from request to delivery.
Burndown: Work remaining in sprint.
Burnup: Work completed over time.

section.name: quality-metrics
section.p: Quality metrics track defect rate and rework:

list.item: Escaped defects: Bugs found in production.
Defect density: Bugs per story point.
Rework rate: Reopened issues percentage.
Test coverage: Code coverage percentage.

section.name: flow-metrics
section.p: Flow metrics expose bottlenecks and process health:

list.item: WIP: Work in progress count.
Flow efficiency: Active time / total time.
Cumulative flow: Status distribution over time.
Aging WIP: Time items spend in each state.

section.name: team-metrics
section.p: Team metrics measure capacity and satisfaction:

list.item: Capacity utilization: Actual vs planned hours.
Focus factor: Productive time ratio.
Team happiness: Satisfaction score.
Predictability: Commitment vs delivery.

section.name: dashboard-types
section.p: Dashboards are audience-specific. Design three: one for the team, one for management, one for executives. Avoid the trap of a single dashboard for all — each audience has different information needs and refresh cadence.

section.name: team-dashboard
section.p: Purpose: Daily team visibility. Audience: developers, scrum master. Refresh: real-time.

list.item: Sprint burndown: remaining work by day.
Sprint board: current sprint items (Kanban view).
Blocker list: filter for blocked/dependencies.
Team workload: issues per assignee (bar chart).
Recent activity: last 24h updates (feed).

section.name: management-dashboard
section.p: Purpose: Sprint/release status. Audience: product owner, managers. Refresh: hourly.

list.item: Sprint progress: percent complete (gauge).
Velocity trend: last 6 sprints (line chart).
Release burnup: scope vs completed (chart).
Risk register: open risks (list).
Milestone timeline: upcoming milestones (timeline).
Blockers summary: blocked items count (count widget).

section.name: executive-dashboard
section.p: Purpose: Portfolio health. Audience: executives, stakeholders. Refresh: daily.

list.item: Portfolio status: all projects health (status grid).
Delivery confidence: on-track percentage (gauge).
Resource allocation: team distribution (pie chart).
Budget vs actual: cost tracking (bar chart).
Key milestones: next 90 days (timeline).
Risk heatmap: impact vs probability (matrix).

section.name: kpi-definitions
section.p: Key Performance Indicators are the subset of metrics you track actively. Keep 5-7 per audience to prevent metric fatigue.

list.item: Velocity: SUM(story_points) WHERE sprint = X AND status = Done. Target: Trending up or stable. Display: line chart of last 10 sprints.
Sprint commitment: completed_points / committed_points × 100. Target: ≥85%. Display: gauge.
Sprint goal achieved: Count where sprint_goal = met. Target: 100%. Display: binary indicator.
Escaped defects: bugs_in_production per sprint. Target: <2. Display: number with trend.
Throughput: COUNT(issues) WHERE completed_date in period. Target: Trending up. Display: bar chart (weekly).
Cycle time: AVG(completed_date - started_date). Target: <5 days. Display: histogram.
WIP compliance: COUNT(issues in_progress) ≤ WIP_limit. Target: 100%. Display: percentage.
Flow efficiency: active_time / (active_time + wait_time) × 100. Target: >40%. Display: gauge.

section.name: dashboard-design-principles
section.p: Design dashboards with information hierarchy and visual consistency:

list.item: Level 1: Overall Health (traffic light red/yellow/green).
Level 2: Key Metrics (absolute numbers).
Level 3: Trend Charts (line/bar graphs).
Level 4: Detailed Data (scrollable tables with drill-down).
Green: On track, no issues.
Yellow: At risk, needs attention.
Red: Off track, requires escalation.
Blue: Information, neutral.
Gray: Not started, N/A.

section.name: widget-layout
section.p: Layout follows a three-tier pattern: main metric at top (large), secondary metric at top-right (medium), primary chart spanning the middle (wide), quick-stats row beneath (three small widgets), detailed table at bottom (scrollable).

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Proven patterns for dashboard design and maintenance:

rule.statement: Every widget must answer "which decision does this enable?" Strip widgets without an answer.
rule.rationale: Widgets without a decision driver become status theater — pretty, ignored, never used for decisions.

rule.statement: Keep top-level dashboards to ≤7 metrics; deeper detail lives one click down.
rule.rationale: More than 7 metrics overload cognitive bandwidth and dilute attention from the critical few.

rule.statement: Tie green/yellow/red thresholds to the team's commitment, not industry averages.
rule.rationale: Generic thresholds produce misleading signals. A threshold is actionable only if the team can influence it.

rule.statement: Always show trend arrows next to absolute numbers; absolute alone is meaningless.
rule.rationale: 50 open issues is either excellent (trending down from 100) or a disaster (trending up from 25). Context matters.

rule.statement: Mobile-test executive dashboards — they get read in elevators and Ubers.
rule.rationale: Executive dashboards will be viewed on smartphones; an untestable responsive design makes them useless at decision time.

rule.statement: Version dashboard configs in git; treat schema changes like code reviews.
rule.rationale: Without version history, dashboards drift silently from reality. Git history provides the audit trail and rollback path.

rule.statement: Know your audience — tailor detail level and refresh cadence.
rule.rationale: Executives need summary + trend; teams need real-time + blockers. One dashboard for all confuses everyone.

rule.statement: Lead with insights, not data. Dashboard title should be "On Track for Q2 Release" not "Velocity = 42".
rule.rationale: Insights drive decisions; raw data requires interpretation and delays action.

rule.statement: Include recommended actions in dashboard context, not just metrics.
rule.rationale: Red status without a next step frustrates stakeholders. Always pair status with recommended action.

rule.statement: Regular cadence — build trust through consistency. Report at the same time, on the same channel, every period.
rule.rationale: Inconsistent reporting trains stakeholders to ignore it. Consistency builds trust and habit.

rule.statement: Review dashboard effectiveness quarterly: Are they read? Do they drive decisions? Retire stale dashboards.
rule.rationale: Dashboards degrade over time as business questions change. Quarterly audits prevent rot.

rule.statement: Avoid vanity metrics (velocity without context, MTTR without incident count). All metrics must drive a decision.
rule.rationale: Vanity metrics optimize teams for the metric, not the outcome, and destroy the signal-to-noise ratio.

rule.statement: Real-time refresh is rarely needed and quietly burns API quota. Refresh team dashboards hourly, management daily, executive weekly.
rule.rationale: Constant refresh adds no value past a certain cadence and multiplies data-source load.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Use a Claude subagent to assemble the dashboard spec (which widgets, which audiences, which thresholds), then generate the queries / API calls / configuration as code. The agent maintains the spec doc; humans approve threshold changes. Run a weekly "dashboard hygiene" subagent that flags widgets that haven't been opened in N days.

list.item: faion-pm-agent — drafts dashboard specs per audience and KPI mapping.
General-purpose Claude subagent — translates spec into Jira JQL, Linear filters, GitHub Projects views.
faion-sdd-executor-agent — stages dashboard config changes through review.

example.title: Dashboard spec generation
example.code: Given audience <executives|managers|team> and goal <X>, propose 5–7 widgets. For each, list: metric formula, data source, refresh cadence, threshold for green/yellow/red, the decision it enables. Reject any widget that has no decision attached.

example.title: Dashboard audit
example.code: Audit dashboards under <path>. For each widget output: last-opened date, decisions made from it in last 90 days, recommendation (KEEP / FIX / RETIRE) with one-line rationale.

antipattern.description: LLMs love to add charts. Constrain by max-widget count per audience.
antipattern.reason: More widgets = more cognitive load; agents default to comprehensive over focused. Set a hard limit in the prompt.

antipattern.description: Agents will fabricate KPI formulas — require explicit data-source field names.
antipattern.reason: LLMs invent plausible-sounding formulas without verifying against actual schema. Always pass the schema as input and validate the output.

antipattern.description: Forecasting from velocity needs ≥6 sprints of data; agents will project from 1–2 if not constrained.
antipattern.reason: Insufficient data produces unreliable forecasts. Explicitly require minimum data window in the prompt.

antipattern.description: Avoid agents auto-filing Jira/Linear changes from dashboard rules; one bad rule cascades broadly.
antipattern.reason: Programmatic actions from dashboard insights create feedback loops and can cause unintended bulk changes. Always route through human approval.

antipattern.description: Ensure the agent labels each metric as leading or lagging; it conflates them silently.
antipattern.reason: Leading (velocity, WIP) vs lagging (escaped defects, rework) metrics serve different purposes. Agents often mix them without distinction, producing misleading dashboards.

list.item: jira-cli — Run JQL, fetch sprint data (brew install ankitpokhrel/jira-cli/jira-cli)
gh — GitHub Projects fields + issue export (brew install gh)
glab — GitLab Boards data export (brew install glab)
linear-cli — Linear cycles, issue exports (https://github.com/evangodon/lr)
metabase-cli — Programmatic Metabase dashboards (pip install metabase-api)
grafanactl — Grafana dashboards as code (https://grafana.com/docs/grafana/latest/cli/)
Jira Dashboards — SaaS, REST API. Native; gadget vocabulary is dated but functional.
Linear Insights — SaaS, GraphQL API. Built-in flow metrics, natively understands cycle time.
Metabase — OSS / SaaS, REST API. Best for SQL-backed dashboards with custom queries.
Grafana — OSS, REST + JSON API. Strong for time-series + DORA-style metrics.
Looker Studio — SaaS, API, free. Ties to Sheets/BigQuery; good for cost-conscious teams.
Hex — SaaS, API. Notebook + dashboard hybrid; strong for exploratory analytics.
Cumul.io / Plotly — SaaS, API. Embedding into product UI; white-label dashboard capability.
GitHub Insights — SaaS, GraphQL API. Engineering-flow visibility on top of Projects.

section.name: failure-modes
section.p: Common pitfalls when implementing reporting systems:

antipattern.description: Reports become "status theater": pretty, ignored, never drive decisions.
antipattern.reason: Dashboards without clear decision points and audience alignment become ornamental. They must answer questions that stakeholders are actually asking.

antipattern.description: Vanity metrics (velocity, MTTR-without-context) lead teams to optimize for the metric, not the outcome.
antipattern.reason: When teams are measured on a metric, they optimize for it. Velocity without outcome context incentivizes inflated estimates; MTTR without incident severity incentivizes fast-but-incomplete fixes.

antipattern.description: Dashboards drift from reality once their data sources change schema.
antipattern.reason: Schema changes in issue trackers break dashboard queries silently. Version dashboard configs and re-validate after every schema change.

antipattern.description: Executive dashboards over-aggregate; root-cause investigation still needs ticket-level access.
antipattern.reason: Aggregation loses signal. An executive dashboard that says "velocity is down" is useless without a path to investigate which issue category or assignee caused the drop.

antipattern.description: Real-time refresh is rarely needed and quietly burns API quota.
antipattern.reason: The cost of constant polling is high; the value past a 1-hour cadence is low. Set refresh appropriately and monitor API usage.
